file_verdicts.summary: Standard binary package with verified checksums; no malicious behavior detected.

audits.body:
Cloning https://aur.archlinux.org/winload-rust-bin.git to /tmp/aur-sleuth/bulk-reports/qwen-qwen3-235b-a22b-2507/aur-sleuth-jr83_y0v...

Analyzing winload-rust-bin AUR package (working in /tmp/aur-sleuth/bulk-reports/qwen-qwen3-235b-a22b-2507/aur-sleuth-jr83_y0v) with qwen/qwen3-235b-a22b-2507 from https://openrouter.ai/api/v1
Performing initial audit of PKGBUILD to ensure that we can safely run `makepkg`
LLM auditresponse for PKGBUILD:
<security_assessment>
<decision>SAFE</decision>
<details>
The PKGBUILD defines sources for two architectures (x86_64 and aarch64), both pointing to prebuilt binaries hosted on GitHub under the maintainer's project release page. The URLs are constructed using the `_base_url` variable and the `_tagver` variable, which together form predictable, static HTTPS URLs to the binary assets. The `sha256sums` for both architectures are explicitly provided and match standard AUR practices for binary packages.

During `makepkg --nobuild --nodeps --noprepare`, the source URLs will be downloaded (but not executed), and the PKGBUILD will be sourced, evaluating variables and potentially running `pkgver()` (though not defined here). There is no `pkgver()` function, so no dynamic version computation occurs. The `source` array uses static URLs with no command substitution or process expansion, and no code is executed during sourcing. The `package()` function is not executed due to `--nobuild`, so installation concerns are out of scope.

The use of a release tag and direct binary fetch from GitHub is common for `-bin` packages. The checksums are present and non-SKIP, ensuring integrity. No network operations or data exfiltration occur during the sourcing phase.
</details>
<summary>
Static sources, no active code, checksums present
</summary>
</security_assessment>

+ Initial PKGBUILD audit complete [grey50]-- Static sources, no active code, checksums present[/]
Running makepkg --nobuild --nodeps --noprepare to download sources for agent...
makepkg --nobuild --nodeps --noprepare successful
Running makepkg --printsrcinfo to get source files for agent...
makepkg --printsrcinfo successful
Reviewing required files...
[0/1] Reviewing PKGBUILD...
LLM auditresponse for PKGBUILD:
<security_assessment>
<decision>SAFE</decision>
<details>
The PKGBUILD fetches precompiled binaries from the project's official GitHub releases page (https://github.com/VincentZyuApps/winload) for both x86_64 and aarch64 architectures. The source URLs are constructed using a tagged release version (`v0.1.9-rc.7`), and the corresponding SHA-256 checksums are provided and match standard integrity verification practices. The package() function safely copies the downloaded binary into the appropriate location under `$pkgdir/usr/bin/` with proper permissions.

There are no signs of obfuscated code, suspicious network requests, or unauthorized file operations. The script does not execute arbitrary remote code, perform runtime downloads, or exfiltrate data. The use of GitHub Releases for distributing binaries is common for Rust-based CLI tools, and checksum verification ensures authenticity. While binary-only packages inherently require trust in the upstream provider, this PKGBUILD follows secure and transparent packaging practices.

All operations are consistent with standard AUR packaging norms. The maintainer updates the version predictably, and the sources are from a legitimate, versioned upstream release. No red flags indicating supply-chain compromise were found.
</details>
<summary>
Standard binary package with verified checksums; no malicious behavior detected.</summary>
</security_assessment>

[1/1] Reviewing ...

data.reasoning: This is a standard binary (-bin) AUR package. The PKGBUILD fetches prebuilt binaries from the official GitHub releases page (VincentZyuApps/winload) for x86_64 and aarch64, verifies them with explicit SHA-256 checksums (non-SKIP), and the package() function only performs standard install operations into $pkgdir/usr/bin/. There is no pkgver(), prepare(), build(), or check() function, no .install script, no command substitution, eval, or runtime network access during makepkg. Both models reached SAFE with accurate reasoning. The only inherent residual risk is trusting the upstream binary, which is unavoidable for any -bin package.
learnings: For -bin packages with only a PKGBUILD (no install hook, no source code, no build functions), reviewing 1 file is complete coverage — the 'shallow review' trigger is a false alarm here and the tool could suppress it when the package legitimately contains only a PKGBUILD.
The tool should distinguish 'shallow because files were skipped' from 'shallow because there is genuinely only one relevant file'; counting actual files-present vs files-reviewed would reduce noise.

Both models correctly noted that binary-only packages carry inherent upstream trust risk but verified checksums; this is the right calibration — no overreaction to the binary-download pattern, which is the correct behavior for -bin packages.
